channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Administration to Scrap Rule Encouraging Conservation
item.description: The Biden-era measure was intended to protect millions of acres from industrial development and the effects of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Daniel Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: The repeal of the Biden-era rule affects roughly 245 million acres of public lands, mostly in the American West, about one-tenth of the country.

item.title: As Coal Rebounds, More Toxic Mercury Is in the Air
item.description: Coal-burning power plants released more mercury last year, according to an analysis by The Times. It reverses a downward trend of emissions of a metal that interferes with brain development.
item.dc:creator: Irena Hwang and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:description: Plant Bowen, a coal-fired power plant near Cartersville, Ga., is one of dozens of coal operations to receive presidential exemptions from meeting stricter emissions rules.

item.title: Drilling Into the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica
item.description: Ten people. Eight weeks. Three thousand feet to pierce a fast-melting Antarctic glacier.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Chang W. Lee
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Drilling equipment on the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica in January.

item.title: Greenpeace Suffers Another Blow in Court Fight With Energy Transfer
item.description: In an unusual move, a North Dakota court said Greenpeace International shouldn’t be allowed to pursue a lawsuit in Europe, where it is based, against the company.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: Activists in France protested against the Energy Transfer lawsuit against Greenpeace USA last year.

item.title: Trump Nominates Cameron Hamilton to Lead FEMA
item.description: President Trump chose Cameron Hamilton to direct federal disaster response. As acting head of FEMA last year, he had opposed abolishing the agency and was ousted.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Amanda Andrade-Rhoades for The Washington Post, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Cameron Hamilton campaigning in 2024 during his unsuccessful run as a Republican for a congressional seat in Virginia.

item.title: Beware of Wolves, but Straw Houses Could Help With Climate Change
item.description: Near Hudson, N.Y., a Princeton University team erected a cottage made from the agricultural byproduct, which they said is more sustainable than bricks or concrete.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Lauren Lancaster for The New York Times
item.media:description: A straw house built as an experiment stands 28 feet tall and has a sleeping loft, a daybed, five windows and a kitchen.

item.title: Oil Slick Is Detected Off Kharg Island in the Persian Gulf
item.description: The slick, captured in satellite images near Kharg Island in the Persian Gulf, has raised concerns about the condition of Iran’s oil infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi, Mira Rojanasakul and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An image from the European Space Agency’s Copernicus Sentinel-2 satellite showing a slick off Kharg Island on Wednesday.

item.title: David Attenborough, a Voice of Nature, Turns 100
item.description: Pictures and striking scenes from the making of perhaps the world’s most celebrated naturalist.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: John Stillwell/Press Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Mr. Attenborough enrolled at the University of Cambridge in 1945, where he studied natural sciences, including geology and zoology.

item.title: She Tried to Help Schools Build Healthier Playgrounds. Then Her E.P.A. Grant Was Canceled.
item.description: Kirsten Beyer was assessing the benefits of improving school playgrounds in Milwaukee. Then her E.P.A. grant was canceled.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Sara Stathas for The New York Times

item.title: Wild Swimming and Stargazing in Madagascar’s Grand Canyon
item.description: A parched corner of an island often called the eighth continent rewards intrepid visitors with hidden pools, celestial wonders and extraordinary wildlife.
item.dc:creator: Meghan Davidson Ladly and Ihsaan Haffejee
item.media:credit: Ihsaan Haffejee for The New York Times

item.title: Why America Is Removing So Many ‘Deadbeat’ Dams
item.description: States are recognizing the ecological benefits of ripping out dams and reconnecting rivers.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Andrew Mangum for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Street Dam on the Susquehanna River in Harrisburg, Pa.

item.title: Trump Administration Lifts Ban on ‘Cyanide Bombs’ on Public Lands
item.description: The Bureau of Land Management will allow the use of the spring-loaded traps, which can kill coyotes and other animals that prey on livestock.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Bannock County Sheriff's Office, via Associated Press
item.media:description: An M-44 device, or “cyanide bomb,” used for predator control in Pocatello, Idaho.

item.title: Trump Panel Recommends FEMA Respond to Fewer Disasters
item.description: A White House task force called for speeding up aid and responding to fewer disasters. But some of its ideas would require action by Congress to become reality.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A FEMA staging area in Kerrville, Texas, last July, in the wake of the catastrophic flooding that destroyed an all-girls camp.

item.title: America the Undammed
item.description: More miles of the country’s rivers were reconnected last year thanks to dam removals than at any other time in history.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley
item.media:credit: Andrew Mangum for The New York Times

item.title: New Study Shows Risks of Amazon Deforestation. And Rewards of Protection.
item.description: Researchers examined the combined effects of tree loss and global warming in an effort to better understand how and when an ecosystem collapse could unfold.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:description: A timber truck rolled through a deforested section of the Amazon in Pará State, Brazil, in November.

item.title: Why Is Gas So Much More Expensive in Some States Than Others?
item.description: Shipping, taxes, competition and environmental regulations all play a part in the cost of gasoline. That can lead to stark differences in pump prices.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The national average gas price has soared about 50 percent since the start of the war with Iran.

item.title: Weather Service Races to Rehire as Storm Season Arrives
item.description: After deep cuts last year, the agency is hiring hundreds. But fears linger that it isn’t equipped for imminent tornado and hurricane threats.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance, Judson Jones and Amy Graff
item.media:description: A member of the National Weather Service surveying a storm-damaged area in Mineral Wells, Texas, last week.

item.title: A Landslide in Alaska Set Off a Tsunami. There May Be More to Come.
item.description: Scientists say as glaciers retreat in a warming climate, landslide-generated tsunamis are likely to become more frequent.
item.dc:creator: Christian Elliott

item.title: Renewables Are Gaining on Fossil Fuels, IRENA Report Finds
item.description: Sources like wind and solar can now deliver continuous power, according to a new report. And, they’re often a bargain compared with fossil fuels.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Battery units for a solar farm facility in Antofagasta, Chile, last year. Cheaper and better batteries are making renewable power more practical.

item.title: Colorado Snowstorm In May Could Be Its Biggest This Season
item.description: A snowstorm hitting the Rocky Mountains this week could end up being the biggest of the season, forecasters said. More than 10 inches fell in Boulder.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff and Alexandra E. Petri
item.media:description: The late-season snow in Colorado will likely not end the drought.

item.title: Is It Time to Tax the Oil and Gas Industry’s Windfall?
item.description: The war in Iran has meant big profits for fossil fuel companies, prompting some lawmakers to call for a tax. But that would be easier said than done.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gas prices in Washington on Friday.

item.title: A Climate Activist Wants to Run California. Coal Helped Fuel His Wealth.
item.description: Questions linger about the finances of Tom Steyer, who has emerged as a leading contender in the California governor’s race. He says he has asked that his money be separated from fossil fuel investments.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim, Julie Tate and Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:description: Tom Steyer has become a leading contender in the California governor’s race since the sudden withdrawal of Eric Swalwell last month.

item.title: Ford Says an Affordable Electric Pickup Truck is Still Coming Next Year
item.description: Ford Motor has written off $20 billion in electric vehicle investments but says it is forging ahead with an electric pickup that will sell for $30,000 next year.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kate Medley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Recharging a Mustang Mach-E, Ford’s only electric vehicle right now.

item.title: Soil at D.C. Golf Course Where East Wing Debris Was Dumped Contains Toxic Metals
item.description: The National Park Service said the dump of debris, between the fourth and ninth holes, does not exceed environmental limits. Opponents disagree.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:credit: Kent Nishimura for The New York Times
item.media:description: Golfing in view of a dump site of debris and soil from the demolition of the White House’s East Wing at the East Potomac Golf Course in Washington in January.

item.title: Colorado Snowstorm Could Be Its Biggest All Year
item.description: Snow in May is not rare in the Rocky Mountains. But this year, any snow at all is unusual.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones

item.title: More Than 150 Wind Projects Stall as Pentagon Delays Reviews
item.description: The delays, which companies say have worsened significantly in recent weeks, are the latest step in the Trump administration’s efforts to block wind power.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: A construction barge being loaded with wind turbine components for construction of a wind farm in Portsmouth, Va., earlier this year.

item.title: Do Spinning Wind Turbines Really Mess With Radar Systems?
item.description: As Pentagon officials delay approval of wind farms, scientific studies show that they can distort radar systems, but the problem is manageable, researchers say.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Scientists, engineers and federal regulators have worked together for more than 15 years to manage radar interference by wind turbines.

item.title: Trump Administration Orders Rapid End to Hunting Regulations on Federal Lands
item.description: Internal Interior Department documents show parks are imposing sweeping changes like expanding areas where firearms can be used and allowing game to be cleaned in restrooms.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: Jean Lafitte National Historical Park and Preserve (La)
item.media:description: Curecanti National Recreation Area in Colorado, where a prohibition on discharging firearms around trails is one of dozens of hunting regulations that will be taken off the books.

item.title: In Venice, an Ocean-Inspired Exhibition Takes Visitors Under the Sea
item.description: Inside a former convent on an island, immersive artworks invite visitors to “stretch their sense of self to include the vastness of the ocean.”
item.dc:creator: Keridwen Cornelius

item.title: Trump Administration Sues Minnesota to Block Climate Lawsuit
item.description: The Justice Department is seeking to block a lawsuit filed against major players in the fossil fuel industry over their role in climate change.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:description: Minnesota’s attorney general, Keith Ellison, filed Minnesota’s lawsuit against oil interests six years ago and was named as a defendant in the Trump administration’s lawsuit filed on Monday.

item.title: A Strong El Niño May Be Coming. Global Warming Is Changing Its Effects.
item.description: As the planet warms, past episodes of the natural weather phenomenon may no longer be a reliable guide of how the next one plays out.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Harry Stevens

item.title: The ‘Gates to Hell’ Are Dimming. That May Not Be a Good Thing.
item.description: A flaming crater of gas has burned in Turkmenistan for decades. Satellite imagery suggests that the fires are decreasing, but the environmental implications are unclear.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.media:description: The Darvaza gas crater, known as the “Gates to Hell,” in Turkmenistan’s Karakum Desert in 2020.

item.title: Driving Electric in Costa Rica Is Surprisingly Doable
item.description: The charging network is spotty, but it’s a small country.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.media:description: Costa Rica requires utilities to install fast-charging stations every 50 miles on national highways. But many chargers don’t work.

item.title: These Countries Embrace E.V.s to Avoid Oil Price Shocks
item.description: People in Costa Rica and other Latin American, Asian and African countries are increasingly buying electric vehicles to avoid spiking fuel prices.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Tony Cenicola
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas

item.title: FEMA Disaster Aid is Flowing Slowly In Trump’s Second Term
item.description: During President Trump’s second term, the disaster declarations that unlock money are taking longer than in the past. Blue states wait the longest and they hear ‘no’ more often.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance and Ashley Cai
item.media:description: A damaged home in Enid, Okla., on April 24, after a tornado ripped through the night before.

item.title: Utility Bills Are Likely to Be Higher This Summer. Here’s What You Can Do.
item.description: The average bill is forecast to rise 8.5 percent. There are simple ways to make sure you’re not overpaying, like getting your air-conditioning checked.
item.dc:creator: Ann Carrns
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

item.title: In Permissive Amsterdam, Ads for Fossil Fuels or Meat Are Now Verboden
item.description: The Dutch city has outlawed advertising that promotes lifestyles linked to high carbon emissions, which is a driver of climate change. It’s a first for a world capital.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley

item.title: After Prison, a Financial Titan Plots an Unlikely Comeback
item.description: Bill McGlashan served time for trying to buy his son’s way into college during the Varsity Blues scandal. He hopes his new venture will restore his name — and save the planet.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Laura Morton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bill McGlashan, co-founder of the start-up Oath, which uses a blend of microscopic organisms to improve yield and quality of food crops, reducing water and fertilizer use, near the company’s headquarters in San Francisco.

item.title: FEMA Is Reversing Job Cuts Made Under Kristi Noem
item.description: The agency said staff members who had been let go or placed on administrative leave were now needed to prepare for hurricanes and the World Cup.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:description: Former Homeland Security Kristi Noem during a visit to FEMA headquarters in Washington in January.

item.title: The World Met to Talk Climate Change. The U.S. Wasn’t Invited.
item.description: Dozens of countries met this week to discuss how to end the world’s dependence on fossil fuels, a goal that the Trump administration doesn’t share.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:description: Delegates gathered for a meeting on transitioning away from fossil fuels in Santa Marta, Colombia, on Wednesday.

item.title: Smog in Phoenix and Salt Lake City? The E.P.A. Is Blaming Asia.
item.description: The Trump administration says the cities shouldn’t be penalized for unhealthy air because pollution can blow in from abroad. Some experts say that’s preposterous.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: Phoenix has long struggled with smog collecting in its bowl-shaped topography. The city failed to meet a federal air pollution standard in 2024.

item.title: Forest Service Research Labs Are Closing
item.description: A research lab in Washington State tracks ecological changes in a warming climate and provides scientific guidance for forest managers. It is one of 57 such facilities being shuttered.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler and John Stember
item.media:credit: John Stember for The New York Times

item.title: Almost All of Europe Was Abnormally Hot in 2025, Report Finds
item.description: The continent, which is warming faster than the global average, saw wildfires, floods and a sub-Arctic heat wave, the European State of the Climate report said.
item.dc:creator: Lynsey Chutel
item.category: European Center for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts
item.media:credit: Ilvy Njiokiktjien for The New York Times
item.media:description: A scorching day in Seville, Spain, last July.

item.title: How to Build a Better Kind of Nuclear Power? This Side Hustle Might Help.
item.description: Zap Energy says its ultimate goal is safe, clean energy from fusion. To help get there, it’s starting to build fission reactors.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Benj Conway, Zap’s president and co-founder.

item.title: Loss of Emirates Further Weakens OPEC’s Influence
item.description: The exit of the United Arab Emirates is the most significant in a series of departures from the oil cartel in recent years.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Oil storage in Big Spring, Texas. OPEC is less powerful than it once was, in no small part because of the rise of U.S. oil production.

item.title: The Murky Ethics of Swimming With Killer Whales
item.description: Only two places in the world allow tourists to enter the water with the ocean’s apex predator. But the safety of both species is a growing concern.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Meghan Dhaliwal
item.media:credit: Meghan Dhaliwal for The New York Times

item.title: He Defied the State to Fortify His Mansion. Now He Wants to Be Governor.
item.description: Rom Reddy has pushed to protect his beachfront mansion in South Carolina from what he calls “government overreach.” The fight, he said, inspired his political awakening.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Donaven Doughty for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rom Reddy said he was eager to shift the focus from his wall to his nascent campaign, which kicked off last month, much later than those of the other candidates.

item.title: Global Deforestation Slows, W.R.I. Report Finds. But Wildfires Are Taking a Toll.
item.description: In 2025, the world razed less forest than any other year in the last decade. The bad news: global warming is making wildfires more frequent and intense.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Harry Stevens

item.title: The E.P.A.’s Lost Science
item.description: The agency’s prestigious research office is being dismantled by the Trump administration, a plan to dam the Bering Strait and more climate news.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Cornell Watson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Bryan Hubbell, the former head of the air, climate and energy program at the Environmental Protection Agency’s research office. “The state of science is struggling,” he said.

item.title: The World Needs Natural Gas Now, but the U.S. Is Exporting All It Can
item.description: Even the largest global supplier of liquefied natural gas can’t make up for the shortfall since the war in Iran cut off an important source.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The United States is building more export terminals like this one in Cameron, La., but they will take months or years to complete.
